question_text: Module 2 opens by describing God's original plan for humanity. According to the teaching, why did God make man?
answer_options: To share the intricate beauty of God's glory as a friend
To populate the earth and multiply across the nations
To labor over the ground and tend the animals alone
To live at a distance from God and worship from afar
correct_answer: To share the intricate beauty of God's glory as a friend

question_text: The module states that man was designed for a particular role on the earth. In simple words, what was man designed to do?
answer_options: To carry God's presence and represent God's authority on the earth
To build cities and establish kingdoms in God's name
To record God's words and pass them to future generations
To remain hidden from creation until God called him forth
correct_answer: To carry God's presence and represent God's authority on the earth

question_text: The module gives a memorable 'Catch this' line about the Image of God in man, breaking it into three parts. According to that line, what do image, likeness, and dominion each represent?
answer_options: Image is identity; likeness is capacity; dominion is assignment
Image is glory; likeness is holiness; dominion is fellowship
Image is identity; likeness is authority; dominion is friendship
Image is capacity; likeness is identity; dominion is reflection
correct_answer: Image is identity; likeness is capacity; dominion is assignment

question_text: The module defines sin in terms of a person's state, not merely their actions. How does the teaching define what sin is?
answer_options: The degenerated state of a man from the image of the Father
A list of wrong actions that break God's written law
The temporary mistakes a believer makes before repentance
A weakness of the body that fades as a person matures
correct_answer: The degenerated state of a man from the image of the Father

question_text: The module names the first created being to embody sin and explains what he did. Who was it, and what did he do?
answer_options: Lucifer, who showed extreme pride and rebellion, desiring to exalt himself above God
Adam, who ate the fruit and attracted God's righteous judgement
Satan, who tricked Eve before he had ever sinned himself
Cain, who rebelled against God's will out of jealousy and anger
correct_answer: Lucifer, who showed extreme pride and rebellion, desiring to exalt himself above God

question_text: Describing the Fall, the module explains how Adam came to sin. According to the teaching, what happened?
answer_options: Satan, through his own self-will, took advantage of the self-choice of man and tricked Adam into sinning
Adam invented sin on his own without any outside influence at all
God withdrew His presence, leaving Adam no choice but to disobey
Adam was forced by Lucifer to eat the fruit against his own will
correct_answer: Satan, through his own self-will, took advantage of the self-choice of man and tricked Adam into sinning

question_text: The module uses the example of children to make a point about sin. It observes that children are inclined to lie or do mischievous things though no one taught them. What does this point to?
answer_options: The inherent nature of sin passed to all men following the rebellion of the first man, Adam
The failure of parents to model good moral behavior early
A passing stage of immaturity that disappears with discipline
The influence of a sinful society shaping a child's choices
correct_answer: The inherent nature of sin passed to all men following the rebellion of the first man, Adam

question_text: The module explains why Jesus came, contrasting it with merely managing sin. According to the teaching, what did Jesus come to do about the nature of sin?
answer_options: To cure the nature of sin by replacing the degenerated DNA of sin and generating a new state of righteousness
To help the old man try harder and gradually improve his behavior
To cover up sin so that God would no longer see man's failures
To manage sin's effects until man could overcome it on his own
correct_answer: To cure the nature of sin by replacing the degenerated DNA of sin and generating a new state of righteousness

question_text: In 'The Great Exchange,' the module insists salvation is more than a powerful, emotional moment. How does the teaching describe what salvation actually is?
answer_options: A permanent change of position and a fact to believe, not a feeling to maintain
A renovation project where a bad person learns to try harder
A second chance to prove yourself worthy through daily consistency
A feeling of excitement that you must work hard to keep alive
correct_answer: A permanent change of position and a fact to believe, not a feeling to maintain

question_text: Under 'What Changed,' the module lists three pillars and a 'Catch this' line tying each to a specific change. According to that line, what does each pillar change?
answer_options: Regeneration changes your inside; justification changes your standing; reconciliation changes your access
Regeneration changes your access; justification changes your inside; reconciliation changes your standing
Regeneration changes your standing; justification changes your access; reconciliation changes your inside
Regeneration changes your feelings; justification changes your behavior; reconciliation changes your status
correct_answer: Regeneration changes your inside; justification changes your standing; reconciliation changes your access
